reply.proposal: Migrate to Express.js (or Fastify if performance is critical) as our HTTP framework, replacing the hand-rolled middleware layer. This gives us native middleware composition patterns, mature middleware ecosystem, and consistent ordering semantics out of the box. We'll wrap existing auth, rate-limiting, and logging logic into middleware functions that plug into the framework's pipeline, then incrementally replace hand-rolled implementations with battle-tested packages (e.g., `express-rate-limit`, `morgan` or `pino-http`). This approach maintains our existing business logic while gaining production-grade middleware ergonomics and clear separation of concerns.

Implement Redux Toolkit as the central state management solution for apps/dashboard, starting with slices for the most critical domains (authentication, user preferences, data views) to avoid over-engineering. We'll set up the store with configureStore, migrate existing context/local state incrementally using thunks for async operations, and add Redux DevTools for debugging. This approach keeps the codebase maintainable while providing a single source of truth and predictable state transitions as feature complexity grows.

We should install Prisma in packages/api and create a schema.prisma file that models our current database structure, then generate the Prisma client for type-safe queries throughout our API layer. I recommend initializing Prisma with `prisma init`, updating the DATABASE_URL in our .env file to point to our existing database, and running `prisma db pull` to introspect the schema rather than writing it from scratch. This gives us immediate type safety on existing queries while allowing us to gradually migrate away from raw SQL strings. We should configure the generated client output in the prisma schema and add prisma generate to our build pipeline.

Use axios as our HTTP client with built-in timeout and retry support via axios-retry middleware, configured at the package level to handle billing API specifics. We'll wrap axios in a dedicated billing client module that implements standardized error handling, logging, and retry policies (exponential backoff for transient failures). This provides type safety if we add TypeScript definitions, maintains consistency with any existing axios usage in packages/api, and gives us transparent observability into billing provider communication without adding heavyweight dependencies.

Store the session token in memory (a JavaScript variable) as the primary store, with an optional sessionStorage backup for page refresh resilience. The SPA should read the token from the Authorization response header after login and manage it in a request interceptor that automatically attaches it as a Bearer token in the Authorization header for all fetch calls. This approach keeps tokens out of localStorage (which is vulnerable to XSS), avoids httpOnly cookies (which the team explicitly wants readable), and gives the SPA full control over token lifecycle. For logout, simply clear the in-memory token; for refresh flows, update it from the response header on each round-trip.
key_technologies: JavaScript fetch API with custom interceptor layer
acknowledged_rejections: httpOnly cookies - rejected because the team explicitly requires JavaScript readability
localStorage - rejected due to XSS vulnerability exposure
custom cookie jar implementation - unnecessary since we're using standard Authorization header

reply.proposal: Install Jest as a dev dependency in packages/api alongside ts-jest for TypeScript support, configure jest.config.js to handle our request handler patterns and any async operations, and set up basic test infrastructure with a tests/ directory structure mirroring our src/ layout. We'll add a test script to package.json and establish conventions for mocking external dependencies (HTTP clients, databases, etc.) using Jest's built-in mocking capabilities. This keeps testing isolated to the API package and allows incremental test coverage without impacting the build or runtime.

I recommend adopting CSS Modules for the marketing pages in apps/dashboard, which provides true component-scoped styling while maintaining native CSS benefits. Each component will have a colocated .module.css file that travels with the component, eliminating style conflicts and making dependencies explicit. This approach aligns with the designer's preference for encapsulation while keeping the build tooling straightforward since we're already using a modern bundler. We can establish a shared tokens file for colors, spacing, and typography to maintain design consistency across pages without sacrificing component isolation.

Implement a distributed Redis cache layer for session data with a write-through pattern: API instances check Redis first before hitting the session store, with automatic invalidation on session updates. Use a consistent hashing strategy to minimize cache misses during instance scaling, and configure a TTL that matches your session expiration policy. This approach reduces database round trips while keeping sessions strongly consistent without requiring cross-instance synchronization complexity.

Implement schema-driven migrations using Alembic as our migration framework, integrated with SQLAlchemy's declarative models as the source of truth. We'll configure Alembic to auto-generate migration files by comparing the current database schema against our Python model definitions, then use Alembic's revision system to track, version, and apply migrations safely. Developers will define schema changes in their models, run `alembic revision --autogenerate` to create migrations, review the generated SQL for correctness, and apply them with `alembic upgrade head`. This eliminates manual SQL writing while maintaining full auditability and the ability to hand-edit migrations when edge cases arise.

Implement a field selection query parameter on the public API endpoints that allows clients to specify exactly which fields to include in the response (e.g., `?fields=id,name,email`). This approach is lightweight, requires minimal backend changes, and provides immediate relief for mobile clients without breaking existing consumers who can continue using full responses. We'll validate the field list against a whitelist per endpoint to prevent information leakage, and document it clearly in the API reference. This avoids the complexity of GraphQL while giving mobile teams control over payload size and network efficiency.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Refactor the HTTP middleware stack into a composable, ordered chain using Hono's built-in middleware utilities and explicit handler factories. Create a single `createMiddlewareStack()` function that assembles auth, logging, and rate limiting in a guaranteed order (logging → rate limit → auth → endpoint), with each middleware as a small, testable Hono middleware function that writes to `c.set()` for state passing. Extract middleware configuration (rate limit tiers, auth schemes, log levels) into a `middleware.config.ts` file that all handlers reference, so policy changes don't require handler rewrites. Add a middleware integration test that validates the ordering and state flow, and document the middleware contract in `packages/api/README.md` so the next engineer knows the canonical order and how to add a new middleware without breaking the stack.
key_technologies: pino for structured logging (already in use)
TypeScript generics for middleware context type safety
acknowledged_rejections: Express or Fastify — we committed to Hono for edge-runtime parity
A third-party middleware orchestration library — one more dependency for a problem we can solve with Hono's context and explicit composition
Runtime middleware registration (plugins loaded from disk) — static composition in one file keeps the order auditable and prevents surprise middleware conflicts

reply.proposal: Do not add Redux Toolkit. Our global state surface is demonstrably small—session, filters, toasts—and Zustand already covers it in ~50 lines per the team decision. Redux Toolkit introduces action creators, reducers, and middleware that add ceremony without solving a real problem here. If state complexity genuinely grows beyond what Zustand handles, we'd first consolidate messy local React state into Zustand slices, then measure again before reconsidering. For now, the ask suggests refactoring existing state, not growing it; audit the dashboard for useState scattered across components and pull it into a new Zustand store instead.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit—the team chose Zustand explicitly to avoid boilerplate on a small state surface; Redux is the opposite direction and would contradict that prior decision

reply.proposal: We should not adopt Prisma. Our team explicitly chose postgres.js with hand-written SQL to keep query plans visible and avoid runtime abstraction layers—a deliberate trade-off we've already made and validated in code. Prisma would reintroduce the abstraction we rejected, add a code-generation step to the build, and complicate our forward-only migration workflow (Prisma's migration engine doesn't map cleanly to numbered SQL files that survive code review). We get type safety today through typed row mappers living next to each query, which are already integrated into our CI and local docker compose setup. If type coverage is the concern, we can strengthen the mapper layer or add a schema validator without the ORM.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM

reply.proposal: Extend the existing native fetch retry wrapper to handle billing provider calls, adding billing-specific error parsing that maps provider error responses into our problem+json envelope. The wrapper already covers retries, timeouts, and JSON handling; we'll add a thin layer that transforms billing errors (like invalid subscription IDs or rate limits from their side) into our RFC 7807 format so error reporting and client handling stay uniform. This keeps us at one HTTP utility, reuses proven patterns, and avoids a new dependency.
acknowledged_rejections: axios or got — we standardized on native fetch with a lightweight wrapper to avoid HTTP client dependencies
dedicated billing SDK — avoids vendor lock and keeps the API surface visible; REST is straightforward enough that the overhead isn't justified
separate error mapping layer — error transformation lives in the retry wrapper to keep billing provider integration colocated

reply.proposal: Keep tokens in httpOnly cookies for security, but have the API server set a parallel non-httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookie with the same token value for JavaScript read access. The SPA reads this mirror cookie and attaches it as an Authorization header on fetch calls, giving the team the explicit control they want while preserving the httpOnly cookie as a fallback. The API accepts tokens from either cookies (automatic) or Authorization headers (explicit), validating both identically. This approach trades a second Set-Cookie for the mobile team's request without weakening the security model—XSS still cannot exfiltrate the httpOnly cookie, and the mirror cookie is no more vulnerable than reading from localStorage would be.
key_technologies: Secure, SameSite=Lax non-httpOnly cookies (new, for JS read)
native fetch with Authorization header injection
acknowledged_rejections: Storing tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage — contradicts the existing httpOnly-only decision and re-introduces XSS exfiltration risk
Removing httpOnly cookies entirely — loses the automatic browser transport and fallback defense
Storing the raw token in a readable cookie and relying on httpOnly for the 'real' one — confusing for operators and opens the readable cookie to XSS anyway

reply.proposal: Don't add Jest. Stick with node:test for API unit tests—it's already the project standard, runs TypeScript output directly, and has zero config overhead. For API handler testing, we can use node:test with a lightweight assertion library (we already have built-ins) and a simple mock helper for Hono context. This keeps the test runner, dependency tree, and CI workflow identical across packages/api and the rest of the monorepo, avoiding the maintenance cost of two test systems.
acknowledged_rejections: Jest — conflicts with established project convention to use node:test; adds a second test framework to maintain; requires separate CI config and tsconfig override; slower startup than node:test for small suites

reply.proposal: Implement an in-memory LRU cache (node-lru-cache) in each API instance with a 5-minute TTL and 10k entry limit, keyed by session token hash. On cache miss, query the UNLOGGED Postgres table; on write (login/logout/refresh), invalidate locally and publish a one-line JSON event to a Postgres LISTEN channel that other instances subscribe to for cache coherence. This keeps the stack at one datastore, avoids Redis operational overhead, and stays within our Node 18+ footprint. Instrument cache hit/miss rates in OpenTelemetry to track effectiveness.
acknowledged_rejections: Redis — adds a second stateful service and contradicts the one-datastore convention; our current Postgres UNLOGGED table is already 15% of Redis latency for reads
Distributed cache invalidation via HTTP gossip — complexity of tracking instance membership and retry logic; Postgres LISTEN is built-in and durable
Memcached — same operational burden as Redis without the structured data types we'd need

reply.proposal: This conflicts with our core decision to keep migrations as hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL that is reviewed as literal database commands. Auto-generated migrations hide the actual schema changes from code review, make it harder to reason about performance implications (indexes, constraints, column ordering), and introduce a tool-specific DSL that obscures what will actually run in production. We've already committed to the overhead of manual migrations as the cost of visibility and control. If the pain point is velocity, the answer is better tooling around our current process (migration templates, schema diffing for review prep) rather than abstracting away the SQL.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma, Drizzle, or TypeORM schema-first with auto-migration generation — violates hand-written SQL review requirement
Flyway or Liquibase with YAML/XML schema specs — introduces a second source of truth and obscures actual SQL being deployed
Supabase/PostgREST schema versioning — adds a managed service dependency outside our Postgres + application boundary

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets using a standard `fields` query parameter (e.g., `GET /v1/users/123?fields=id,name,email`) validated by Zod schemas at the boundary. Each endpoint's OpenAPI spec documents available fields, and the postgres.js queries remain hand-written SQL—we'll use simple column selection logic in the mapper layer to drop unwanted fields from the typed row before JSON serialization. This adds zero dependencies, keeps queries visible, and integrates cleanly with our existing error envelope and OpenAPI tooling. The feature flag can gate rollout per API key tier if needed.
key_technologies: OpenAPI spec generator (document available fields per endpoint)
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — adds a second query language, requires a new executor, and breaks our REST + OpenAPI contract; we committed to REST for straightforward HTTP caching and broad client tooling
JSON:API — overkill for our API surface and would require rewriting all existing endpoints; sparse fieldsets via query params achieves the same goal with one-line parameter additions
Partial response library (like google-api-client's `fields`) — introduces another dependency when Zod + inline mappers handle it in existing code
Database-layer projection — tempting but queries stay hand-written SQL for visibility; filtering columns in the application mapper keeps the query plan unchanged and auditable

reply.proposal: Refactor the middleware stack into a composable, ordered pipeline using Hono's built-in middleware system and our existing integrations. Create a single middleware chain that runs auth → rate limiting → request logging → business logic, with each middleware wrapped in a standard error handler and exported as named exports so call sites are explicit about what they're applying. Adopt Hono's `c.set()` for passing authenticated context (user ID, API key metadata, rate limit state) through the request, and use Hono's `onError` hook to centralize error responses into the problem+json envelope. This eliminates hand-rolled ordering logic, makes the middleware stack testable in isolation with node:test, and leverages Hono's designed-for-this model instead of fighting it.
key_technologies: Hono (existing; using middleware composition and c.set context)
Zod (existing; reuse for input validation at middleware boundaries)
pino (existing; structured logging via Hono middleware)
acknowledged_rejections: Express or Fastify middleware ecosystem — we committed to Hono; switching would repeat the 2026-03 Express evaluation waste
Middleware libraries like passport or helmet — Hono's minimal approach covers our auth and rate limiting; adding opinionated libraries reintroduces the dependency surface we rejected Express for
Custom middleware factory pattern without Hono primitives — hand-rolled composition is what created the current mess; Hono's c.set and native ordering are the fix

reply.proposal: We are not adopting Prisma. The decision log records that Prisma was evaluated in the 2026-02 spike and rejected because the query engine adds cold-start latency on our serverless deploys—a constraint that has not changed—and it hides generated SQL, making query plans invisible during performance debugging. We committed to postgres.js with hand-written SQL and typed row mappers colocated with queries. If type safety for SQL is the concern, we can add sql.d.ts generation from our schema without introducing a runtime abstraction layer, or migrate individual queries to a query builder like Kysely that does not add cold-start overhead. The current approach trades some boilerplate for visibility and cold-start predictability, which the team has already weighted and chosen.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; rejected in 2026-02 spike

reply.proposal: Extend the existing native fetch wrapper to handle the billing provider calls, adding retry logic with exponential backoff, request/response timeouts, and structured error logging via pino. The wrapper already covers retries, timeouts, and JSON handling; we add billing-specific error classification (e.g., rate limits, temporary outages, permanent failures) so that error logs surface which billing errors are transient vs. fatal, and Zod schemas validate the billing provider's response shape at the boundary. This keeps the billing integration consistent with how we handle all outbound HTTP, avoids a new dependency, and makes retry behavior auditable in one place.
key_technologies: exponential backoff (hand-written in the retry wrapper)
acknowledged_rejections: axios — interceptor chains and implicit retry logic made debugging harder in 2026-01; native fetch + explicit wrapper remains simpler
undici (native HTTP client) — Node's fetch is sufficient; undici adds a dependency for marginal gains on our request volume
dedicated billing SDK — Locks us to one provider; REST + our wrapper gives us flexibility to swap providers without rewrites

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly cookies and continue sending them via the browser's automatic cookie attachment—do not expose tokens to JavaScript. If the mobile web team needs explicit token access for specific use cases (e.g., WebSocket authentication where cookies don't auto-attach), implement a scoped `GET /v1/session/token` endpoint that returns a short-lived (5-minute) access token with limited scope, separate from the httpOnly session cookie. The SPA uses the short-lived token only for those specific channels and continues relying on cookies for all standard REST calls. This preserves the 2026-04 pentest finding that localStorage/sessionStorage tokens are vulnerable to XSS exfiltration via compromised scripts (like the analytics snippet that was discovered), while unblocking legitimate technical needs without expanding the attack surface.
key_technologies: Hono middleware for rate limiting the token endpoint (existing)
acknowledged_rejections: Storing tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage — XSS exfiltration demonstrated in 2026-04 pentest; rejected decision stands
Making all session tokens readable to JavaScript — Violates the httpOnly cookie security model that passed the pentest

This preserves our one-datastore, one-framework principle: Tailwind is already in the stack, tree-shakes unused utilities, and pairs seamlessly with React Server Components that the marketing pages may adopt. Component organization itself provides the scoping the designer wants—utilities stay visually adjacent to JSX without splitting files.
key_technologies: CSS custom properties for semantic tokens (via Tailwind theme config)

new CSS-in-JS or scoped-CSS tool — adds vendor risk and build complexity when Tailwind already covers component-level organization and theming

reply.proposal: Implement an in-process LRU cache (node-lru-cache or similar) in front of the Postgres UNLOGGED session table, with cache invalidation via a simple pub/sub pattern using Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY on session mutation events. Each API instance maintains its own cache; when a session is created, updated, or revoked, a trigger publishes a notification that clears the entry from all connected instances' caches. This keeps us at one datastore, avoids Redis operational overhead, and trades sub-millisecond stale-read windows (bounded by the LISTEN latency, typically <10ms) for measurable latency reduction on the hot path. We size the cache to hold ~10k sessions (typical concurrent users × safety margin) and monitor hit rates in OpenTelemetry traces to validate the trade-off.
acknowledged_rejections: Redis — already rejected as a second datastore until 10x traffic scale; this proposal keeps the single-datastore commitment
in-process memory cache without invalidation — already rejected for breaking under multiple instances; this proposal adds Postgres-driven invalidation to make it safe

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets via a standard `fields` query parameter that filters response JSON to only requested fields, validated against Zod schemas per endpoint. Each endpoint schema becomes the allowlist—clients pass `?fields=id,name,email` and middleware strips unrequested keys before serialization. This avoids the resolver complexity that made us reject GraphQL, keeps the REST+OpenAPI contract intact for all clients, and gives mobile teams precise control without a second API style. Implement in the Hono middleware layer so it applies uniformly; document the pattern in OpenAPI as optional query parameters per endpoint.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity still outweigh flexibility; sparse fieldsets solve the stated overfetching problem within the REST boundary we chose
tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; sparse fieldsets keep the contract language-agnostic

This avoids XSS exposure while giving the SPA explicit control over header attachment; the browser still transports the cookie for validation server-side, and the endpoint can gate the token read with rate limiting and referer checks if needed. This is a minimal contract change—one new endpoint—and does not compromise the security posture that rejected localStorage after the 2026-04 pentest.
acknowledged_rejections: Storing tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage — contradicts the 2026-04 pentest findings and prior rejection of XSS exfiltration risk
Exposing the raw token directly in a response cookie readable by JavaScript — defeats the security boundary of httpOnly
Memory-based token store on the client — does not survive page reloads or tab navigation, and creates inconsistent UX across client tabs

reply.proposal: Implement a session data cache in the Postgres UNLOGGED table we already use for session lookups, but add a secondary in-process LRU cache (using a simple Map with TTL) on each API instance to serve the hottest sessions without hitting the database on every request. The in-process cache is local to each instance so no sticky sessions constraint, and we invalidate it via a lightweight broadcast mechanism (polling a version counter in the UNLOGGED table every 30s or event-driven via application logic when sessions update). This gives us sub-millisecond hits for frequently accessed sessions while staying within our one-datastore constraint and keeping ops complexity minimal until we hit the 10x scale trigger for a dedicated cache layer.
key_technologies: Node.js Map or similar in-process LRU cache library (e.g., lru-cache)
TTL-based cache invalidation polling or application-triggered invalidation
acknowledged_rejections: Redis — Rejected per prior ops budget decision to stay at one datastore until 10x traffic triggers reevaluation
Distributed in-memory cache (Memcached) — Same ops burden as Redis; not a datastore decision
Sticky sessions — Rejected as a deploy constraint in the original session caching decision

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets via a query parameter (e.g., `?fields=id,name,email`) that lets clients request only the columns they need from each resource, parsed and validated against an allowlist per endpoint defined in the OpenAPI spec. This keeps the API REST-native, leverages our existing URL versioning and HTTP caching infrastructure, and requires no new dependencies—just response filtering logic in each handler. The mobile team gains bandwidth savings immediately without a platform redesign, and we avoid GraphQL's resolver complexity that we already rejected.
key_technologies: OpenAPI spec extensions (defining allowed fields per endpoint)
query parameter parsing and validation middleware
response serialization filtering (language-native, no new library required)
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — N+1 resolver risks and cache invalidation complexity already ruled out; sparse fieldsets achieve the core overfetch fix within REST
tRPC — Public API must remain language-agnostic; TypeScript lock-in rejected for consumer base
